name: Physics - Mechanics
description: An algebra- and calculus-ready course covering classical mechanics including kinematics, Newton's laws, energy, momentum, rotational motion, simple harmonic motion, and introductory wave physics.

modules.title: Kinematics in 1D
modules.objectives: Define and distinguish displacement, velocity, and acceleration; Apply the four kinematic equations to uniformly accelerated motion; Interpret position-time and velocity-time graphs; Solve free-fall problems using g = 9.8 m/s².
modules.readingNote: OpenStax College Physics 2e — Chapter 2: Kinematics in One Dimension
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Average Velocity for Constant Acceleration
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Flipping Physics — Introduction to 1D Kinematics
modules.assignment: A car starts from rest and accelerates uniformly at 3.0 m/s² for 8.0 s. Calculate (a) the final velocity, (b) the displacement, and (c) the velocity at t = 5.0 s. Then sketch the corresponding v-t graph and the x-t graph, explaining the physical meaning of the slope and area under each curve.

modules.title: Kinematics in 2D: Projectile Motion
modules.objectives: Resolve two-dimensional motion into independent horizontal and vertical components; Apply kinematic equations separately to x- and y-directions; Solve for range, maximum height, and time of flight of a projectile; Analyze the effect of launch angle on range.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax College Physics 2e — Chapter 3: Two-Dimensional Kinematics
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Flipping Physics — Projectile Motion Introduction
modules.assignment: A ball is launched from ground level at 25 m/s at 37° above the horizontal. Calculate (a) the maximum height, (b) the total time of flight, (c) the horizontal range, and (d) the velocity vector (magnitude and direction) at the moment of impact. Show all component analysis clearly.

modules.title: Newton's Laws of Motion
modules.objectives: State Newton's three laws of motion and apply them to single objects and systems; Draw and analyze free-body diagrams; Apply Newton's second law (ΣF = ma) to calculate acceleration; Distinguish between mass and weight and solve Atwood machine problems.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax College Physics 2e — Chapter 4: Dynamics: Force and Newton's Laws
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Newton's First Law of Motion
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Bozeman Science — Newton's Laws of Motion
modules.assignment: Draw a complete free-body diagram for a 5.0 kg block being pulled horizontally at constant velocity by a rope at 30° above horizontal with a tension of 20 N on a rough surface. Calculate the coefficient of kinetic friction, the normal force, and the net force. Then solve a connected two-mass Atwood machine problem for the acceleration and tension.

modules.title: Friction & Inclined Planes
modules.objectives: Distinguish between static and kinetic friction and apply their respective coefficients; Resolve forces into components parallel and perpendicular to an inclined plane; Solve dynamics problems involving blocks on ramps with and without friction; Determine the minimum force required to start moving an object.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax College Physics 2e — Chapter 5: Further Applications of Newton's Laws
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Inclined Plane Force Components
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Flipping Physics — Friction on an Incline
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Inclined Planes and Friction Practice
modules.assignment: A 10.0 kg box rests on a ramp inclined at 25° to the horizontal. The coefficient of static friction is 0.45 and kinetic friction is 0.30. (a) Determine if the box slides. (b) If it does slide, calculate the acceleration. (c) Calculate the minimum applied force parallel to the ramp needed to push the box up the incline at constant velocity.

modules.title: Work & Energy
modules.objectives: Calculate work done by constant and variable forces; Define kinetic energy and apply the work-energy theorem; Distinguish between conservative and nonconservative forces; Calculate power and relate it to work and time.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax College Physics 2e — Chapter 7: Work, Energy, and Energy Resources
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to Work and Energy
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Bozeman Science — Work, Energy, and Power
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Work and Energy Practice
modules.assignment: A 2.0 kg block starts from rest and is pushed 4.0 m along a horizontal surface by a 15 N force at 20° below the horizontal. If μk = 0.25, (a) calculate the work done by each force on the block, (b) find the net work, and (c) determine the final speed using the work-energy theorem. Then calculate the average power if this took 3.0 s.

modules.title: Conservation of Energy
modules.objectives: State and apply the law of conservation of mechanical energy; Calculate gravitational potential energy and elastic potential energy; Apply energy conservation to solve problems involving pendulums, springs, and ramps; Identify when mechanical energy is not conserved and account for energy losses.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax College Physics 2e — Chapter 7: Conservation of Energy
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Flipping Physics — Conservation of Energy with a Spring
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Conservation of Energy Practice
modules.assignment: A roller coaster car of mass 500 kg starts from rest at a height of 40 m. (a) Using conservation of energy, find the speed at the bottom of the first drop. (b) Find the speed at a second hill of height 15 m. (c) If friction does −8000 J of work on the car from start to the second hill, find the new speed at the second hill. Explain why the answer to (c) is lower.

modules.title: Linear Momentum & Impulse
modules.objectives: Define linear momentum and impulse; Apply the impulse-momentum theorem; Explain Newton's second law in terms of the rate of change of momentum; Solve problems involving variable forces using impulse as the area under an F-t graph.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax College Physics 2e — Chapter 8: Linear Momentum and Collisions
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Bozeman Science — Impulse and Momentum
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Momentum and Impulse Practice
modules.assignment: A 0.15 kg baseball moving at 40 m/s is hit by a bat and leaves at 55 m/s in the opposite direction. (a) Calculate the change in momentum (impulse). (b) If the ball is in contact with the bat for 1.5 ms, what is the average force exerted on the ball? (c) Sketch the F-t graph and explain how the area represents the impulse.

modules.title: Conservation of Momentum & Collisions
modules.objectives: State and apply the law of conservation of momentum to isolated systems; Distinguish between elastic and perfectly inelastic collisions; Solve 1D collision problems using conservation of momentum and, where applicable, conservation of kinetic energy; Calculate the velocity of the center of mass of a system.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Elastic and Inelastic Collisions
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Flipping Physics — Elastic and Inelastic Collisions
modules.assignment: Cart A (2.0 kg, moving right at 4.0 m/s) collides with Cart B (3.0 kg, at rest). (a) If the collision is perfectly inelastic, find the final velocity and calculate the kinetic energy lost. (b) If the collision is elastic, find the final velocities of both carts. (c) Compare the two scenarios and explain the physical meaning of the kinetic energy difference.

modules.title: Circular Motion
modules.objectives: Define centripetal acceleration and centripetal force; Apply Newton's second law to uniform circular motion problems; Solve problems involving cars on banked and unbanked curves; Analyze vertical circular motion and identify conditions for maintaining contact at the top of a loop.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax College Physics 2e — Chapter 6: Uniform Circular Motion and Gravitation
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Flipping Physics — Centripetal Force and Circular Motion
modules.assignment: A car of mass 1200 kg travels at 20 m/s around a flat horizontal circular turn of radius 80 m. (a) Calculate the centripetal acceleration and centripetal force required. (b) Identify which force provides the centripetal force and find the required coefficient of friction. (c) Determine the minimum speed for a roller-coaster car to maintain contact at the top of a loop of radius 12 m.

modules.title: Gravitation
modules.objectives: State Newton's law of universal gravitation and apply it to calculate gravitational force; Relate gravitational field strength to the universal law; Derive and apply Kepler's three laws of planetary motion; Calculate orbital speed, period, and radius for circular orbits.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax College Physics 2e — Chapter 6: Gravitation and Kepler's Laws
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Newton's Law of Gravity
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Bozeman Science — Gravitational Force and Orbits
modules.assignment: A satellite orbits Earth at an altitude of 400 km above Earth's surface (Earth's radius = 6.37 × 10⁶ m, M_Earth = 5.97 × 10²⁴ kg, G = 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg²). Calculate (a) the orbital radius, (b) the orbital speed, (c) the orbital period, and (d) compare this to the given orbital period of the ISS (≈92 min) and explain any discrepancy.

modules.title: Torque & Rotational Motion
modules.objectives: Define torque and calculate it using τ = rF sinθ; Apply the conditions for static and rotational equilibrium; Define rotational inertia (moment of inertia) for common geometries; Apply Newton's second law for rotation: τ_net = Iα.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax College Physics 2e — Chapter 9: Statics and Torque
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Bozeman Science — Torque and Rotational Inertia
modules.assignment: A uniform beam of mass 30 kg and length 6.0 m is supported by a pivot at its left end and a vertical cable at 4.5 m from the left end. A 50 kg person stands at 5.0 m from the left end. (a) Draw a free-body diagram. (b) Apply rotational equilibrium to find the cable tension. (c) Find the pivot reaction force. Then calculate the angular acceleration if the cable is cut.

modules.title: Rotational Kinetic Energy & Angular Momentum
modules.objectives: Calculate rotational kinetic energy using KE_rot = ½Iω²; Apply conservation of energy to rolling without slipping; Define angular momentum and apply its conservation to rotating systems; Explain why angular velocity changes when the moment of inertia changes.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax College Physics 2e — Chapter 10: Rotational Motion and Angular Momentum
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Flipping Physics — Rotational Kinetic Energy and Rolling
modules.assignment: A solid sphere (mass 2.0 kg, radius 0.10 m, I = 2/5 MR²) rolls without slipping from rest down a ramp of height 0.80 m. (a) Using conservation of energy, find the speed of the center of mass at the bottom. (b) Find the angular velocity at the bottom. (c) A figure skater with moment of inertia 4.0 kg·m² spins at 2.0 rev/s and pulls arms in to reduce I to 1.5 kg·m²; find the new angular velocity.

modules.title: Simple Harmonic Motion
modules.objectives: Describe the conditions for simple harmonic motion (SHM); Derive and apply expressions for period of a spring-mass system and a pendulum; Calculate position, velocity, and acceleration as functions of time in SHM; Apply energy conservation to SHM to find maximum speed and amplitude.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax College Physics 2e — Chapter 16: Oscillatory Motion and Waves
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to Harmonic Motion
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Bozeman Science — Simple Harmonic Motion
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Simple Harmonic Motion Practice
modules.assignment: A 0.50 kg mass is attached to a spring with k = 200 N/m and displaced 8.0 cm from equilibrium. (a) Find the period and frequency of oscillation. (b) Find the maximum speed and maximum acceleration. (c) Using energy conservation, find the speed when the displacement is 4.0 cm. (d) Predict how the period would change if the mass were doubled, and verify with the formula.

modules.title: Waves & Sound Basics
modules.objectives: Distinguish transverse from longitudinal waves and identify examples of each; Apply the wave equation v = fλ to solve problems; Describe the Doppler effect and calculate observed frequency for moving source or observer; Explain the principle of superposition and conditions for constructive and destructive interference.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax College Physics 2e — Chapter 16–17: Waves and Sound
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Bozeman Science — Wave Properties and Doppler Effect
modules.assignment: A sound source emits a frequency of 400 Hz. (a) If the source moves toward a stationary observer at 30 m/s (speed of sound = 343 m/s), calculate the observed frequency. (b) If the source is stationary but the observer moves away at 30 m/s, calculate the observed frequency. (c) Explain using diagrams the physical reason for the frequency shift in each case.

quizQuestions.question: A car accelerates from rest at 4.0 m/s². What is its velocity after 5.0 s?
On a velocity-time graph, the slope represents:
quizQuestions.explanation: The slope of a v-t graph equals the rate of change of velocity, which is acceleration.

quizQuestions.question: An object is dropped from rest. How far does it fall in 3.0 s? (g = 9.8 m/s²)
Which kinematic equation is most useful when time is unknown?
quizQuestions.explanation: v² = v₀² + 2ax directly relates initial/final velocity and displacement without requiring time.

quizQuestions.question: An object moves at constant velocity. Its acceleration is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Constant velocity means no change in speed or direction, so acceleration is zero by definition.

quizQuestions.question: At the highest point of a projectile's trajectory, which statement is true?
options: Both vx and vy are zero
quizQuestions.explanation: At maximum height, the vertical component of velocity is zero, but the horizontal component remains constant throughout the flight.

quizQuestions.question: A ball is thrown horizontally at 10 m/s from a cliff 20 m high. How long does it take to hit the ground?
quizQuestions.explanation: Using y = ½gt²: 20 = ½(10)t², t² = 4.0, t = 2.0 s (using g ≈ 10 m/s² for simplicity).

quizQuestions.question: Which launch angle gives the maximum horizontal range for a projectile launched from and landing at the same height?
quizQuestions.explanation: Range is maximized at 45° because it balances the horizontal component (cos θ) and the time of flight, which depends on the vertical component (sin θ).

quizQuestions.question: For a projectile (no air resistance), the horizontal acceleration is:
options: 9.8 m/s² in the direction of motion
quizQuestions.explanation: In the absence of air resistance, no horizontal force acts on a projectile, so its horizontal acceleration is zero and horizontal velocity is constant.

quizQuestions.question: A projectile is launched at 30° and another at 60° with the same initial speed. Their ranges are:
options: The 30° launch has a longer range
The 60° launch has a longer range
Not comparable without knowing initial speed
quizQuestions.explanation: Complementary angles (that sum to 90°, like 30° and 60°) give equal horizontal ranges when launched from and landing at the same height.

quizQuestions.question: Newton's first law states that an object remains at rest or in uniform motion unless:
options: It is on a frictionless surface
Acted upon by a net external force
quizQuestions.answer: Acted upon by a net external force
quizQuestions.explanation: Newton's first law (law of inertia) states that an object's state of motion only changes when a net external force acts on it.

quizQuestions.question: A 10 kg object experiences a net force of 30 N. What is its acceleration?
Newton's third law states that for every action there is:
options: A larger reaction in the same direction
An equal and opposite reaction on the same object
An equal and opposite reaction on a different object
No reaction if the objects are not in contact
quizQuestions.answer: An equal and opposite reaction on a different object
quizQuestions.explanation: Newton's third law action-reaction forces are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, but act on different objects.

quizQuestions.question: A 5.0 kg book rests on a table. What is the normal force exerted by the table on the book? (g = 9.8 m/s²)
quizQuestions.explanation: The normal force equals the book's weight: N = mg = (5.0)(9.8) = 49 N.

quizQuestions.question: In an Atwood machine, two masses hang over a frictionless pulley. If M₁ > M₂, the system accelerates with:
quizQuestions.explanation: Applying Newton's second law to both masses: net force = (M₁ − M₂)g, total mass = M₁ + M₂, giving a = (M₁ − M₂)g / (M₁ + M₂).

quizQuestions.question: The maximum static friction force is described by:
options: fs = mg cosθ only on ramps
quizQuestions.explanation: Static friction can range from zero up to its maximum value μs N; it equals exactly μs N only at the verge of sliding.

quizQuestions.question: A block on an inclined plane at angle θ. The component of gravity parallel to the slope is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Resolving gravity along the slope: the component parallel to the incline is mg sinθ (directed down the slope).

quizQuestions.question: The normal force on a block resting on an incline of angle θ equals:
quizQuestions.explanation: The normal force is perpendicular to the incline surface and balances the component of gravity perpendicular to the surface: N = mg cosθ.

quizQuestions.question: Which friction force acts on a block that is already sliding?
quizQuestions.explanation: Once an object is in motion, kinetic (sliding) friction acts, which is generally less than maximum static friction.

quizQuestions.question: A block sits on a ramp. Increasing the angle θ from 0° to 90° causes the normal force to:
quizQuestions.explanation: N = mg cosθ; as θ increases from 0° to 90°, cosθ decreases from 1 to 0, so the normal force decreases from mg to 0.

quizQuestions.question: Work done by a force is calculated as:
quizQuestions.explanation: Work = Force × displacement × cos(angle between force and displacement): W = Fd cosθ.

quizQuestions.question: A force perpendicular to the displacement of an object does:
quizQuestions.explanation: W = Fd cos90° = 0. A force perpendicular to motion (like the normal force) does no work.

quizQuestions.question: The kinetic energy of a 4.0 kg object moving at 6.0 m/s is:
The work-energy theorem states that the net work done on an object equals:
quizQuestions.explanation: The work-energy theorem: W_net = ΔKE = KE_f − KE_i.

quizQuestions.question: A machine does 1200 J of work in 4.0 s. What is its power output?
The gravitational potential energy of a 2.0 kg mass at a height of 5.0 m is: (g = 10 m/s²)
A ball is released from rest at height h. Using conservation of energy, its speed at the bottom is:
quizQuestions.explanation: mgh = ½mv², solving for v: v = √(2gh).

quizQuestions.question: Elastic potential energy stored in a spring compressed by x is:
quizQuestions.explanation: The elastic potential energy of a spring follows Hooke's law: PE = ½kx², where k is the spring constant.

quizQuestions.question: When friction is present, the total mechanical energy of a sliding block:
options: Decreases because friction is a nonconservative force
Stays constant because energy is conserved
quizQuestions.answer: Decreases because friction is a nonconservative force
quizQuestions.explanation: Friction converts mechanical energy to thermal energy, so total mechanical energy (KE + PE) decreases.

quizQuestions.question: A pendulum swings from rest at its highest point. At the lowest point:
options: KE is maximum and PE is minimum
KE is minimum and PE is maximum
Both KE and PE are zero
quizQuestions.answer: KE is maximum and PE is minimum
quizQuestions.explanation: At the lowest point of a pendulum's swing, all gravitational PE has converted to KE, making KE maximum and PE minimum.

quizQuestions.question: The momentum of a 2.0 kg object moving at 8.0 m/s is:
Impulse has the same units as:
quizQuestions.explanation: Impulse = FΔt [N·s = kg·m/s], which are the same units as momentum.

quizQuestions.question: The impulse-momentum theorem states:
quizQuestions.explanation: The impulse-momentum theorem: the net impulse on an object equals the change in its momentum (J = Δp).

quizQuestions.question: A 0.10 kg ball's velocity changes from +20 m/s to −20 m/s. The magnitude of the impulse is:
Increasing the collision time in a car crash (e.g., via airbags) reduces the:
quizQuestions.explanation: Since J = FΔt is fixed (same momentum change), increasing Δt decreases F. Airbags extend collision time to reduce peak force.

quizQuestions.question: Conservation of momentum applies when:
options: The net external force on the system is zero
quizQuestions.answer: The net external force on the system is zero
quizQuestions.explanation: Momentum is conserved in an isolated system (zero net external force), regardless of the nature of internal forces.

quizQuestions.question: In a perfectly inelastic collision:
options: The objects bounce off each other
quizQuestions.explanation: In a perfectly inelastic collision, the colliding objects stick together and move with a common final velocity; kinetic energy is not conserved.

quizQuestions.question: A 3.0 kg cart moving at 4.0 m/s collides and sticks with a 1.0 kg stationary cart. What is their final velocity?
quizQuestions.explanation: Using momentum conservation: (3.0)(4.0) = (3.0 + 1.0)v_f; v_f = 12/4 = 3.0 m/s.

quizQuestions.question: In a perfectly elastic collision, which quantities are both conserved?
quizQuestions.explanation: Elastic collisions conserve both total momentum and total kinetic energy of the system.

quizQuestions.question: If two identical balls undergo a perfectly elastic head-on collision, and ball A was moving while ball B was at rest, afterwards:
options: Both balls move at half the original speed
Ball A continues and ball B stays still
Ball A stops and ball B moves at the original speed of A
Both balls move in opposite directions
quizQuestions.answer: Ball A stops and ball B moves at the original speed of A
quizQuestions.explanation: For an elastic collision between equal masses where one is initially at rest, the moving object stops and the stationary one moves with the original velocity.

quizQuestions.question: Centripetal acceleration always points:
options: Toward the center of the circular path
Away from the center of the circular path
quizQuestions.answer: Toward the center of the circular path
quizQuestions.explanation: Centripetal acceleration is always directed toward the center of the circular path, causing the continuous change in direction of velocity.

quizQuestions.question: The centripetal force on an object in circular motion is provided by:
options: A special outward force called centrifugal force
Whatever net inward force exists in that physical situation
quizQuestions.answer: Whatever net inward force exists in that physical situation
quizQuestions.explanation: Centripetal force is not a separate force; it is the net inward force provided by whatever forces are present (tension, friction, gravity, normal force).

quizQuestions.question: A car of mass m travels at speed v around a flat circular track of radius r. The centripetal force required is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Centripetal force F_c = mv²/r, directed toward the center of the circular path.

quizQuestions.question: At the top of a vertical loop, the minimum speed to maintain contact with the track occurs when:
options: Normal force is at its maximum
quizQuestions.explanation: At minimum speed at the top of a loop, the normal force goes to zero and gravity alone provides the centripetal force: mg = mv²/r, so v_min = √(gr).

quizQuestions.question: If the speed of an object in circular motion doubles while the radius remains constant, the centripetal force:
quizQuestions.explanation: F_c = mv²/r; doubling v increases v² by a factor of 4, so F_c quadruples.

quizQuestions.question: Newton's law of universal gravitation states that the gravitational force between two masses is:
options: Directly proportional to both masses and inversely proportional to distance
Directly proportional to both masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance
quizQuestions.answer: Directly proportional to both masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance
quizQuestions.explanation: F = Gm₁m₂/r²; force is proportional to each mass and inversely proportional to the square of the separation distance.

quizQuestions.question: If the distance between two masses is doubled, the gravitational force between them:
quizQuestions.explanation: Since F ∝ 1/r², doubling r gives F' = F/(2)² = F/4; the force decreases to one-quarter.

quizQuestions.question: Kepler's third law states that the square of a planet's orbital period is proportional to:
options: The cube of its average orbital radius
quizQuestions.answer: The cube of its average orbital radius
quizQuestions.explanation: Kepler's third law: T² ∝ r³ (or T² = (4π²/GM)r³), derived from combining Newton's gravitational law with circular orbit dynamics.

quizQuestions.question: A satellite in a higher orbit compared to one in a lower orbit has:
options: Greater orbital speed and shorter period
Lower orbital speed and longer period
Greater orbital speed and longer period
quizQuestions.answer: Lower orbital speed and longer period
quizQuestions.explanation: From v = √(GM/r), orbital speed decreases with increasing r; from T² ∝ r³, period increases with r.

quizQuestions.question: The gravitational field strength g at distance r from Earth's center is given by:
quizQuestions.explanation: Gravitational field strength g = F/m = GM_Earth/r², which equals the free-fall acceleration at that location.

quizQuestions.question: Torque is calculated as τ = rF sinθ, where θ is the angle between:
options: The position vector r and the force F
The torque and the angular velocity
quizQuestions.answer: The position vector r and the force F
quizQuestions.explanation: Torque τ = r × F; the magnitude is rF sinθ, where θ is the angle between the position vector (from pivot to point of force application) and the force vector.

quizQuestions.question: For an object to be in static equilibrium, which TWO conditions must be satisfied?
quizQuestions.explanation: Static equilibrium requires both translational equilibrium (net force = 0) and rotational equilibrium (net torque = 0).

quizQuestions.question: The moment of inertia of an object depends on:
options: Both its mass and how that mass is distributed relative to the rotation axis
quizQuestions.answer: Both its mass and how that mass is distributed relative to the rotation axis
quizQuestions.explanation: Moment of inertia I = Σmr² depends on the total mass AND how far each mass element is from the rotation axis.

quizQuestions.question: Newton's second law for rotation is:
quizQuestions.explanation: The rotational analog of F = ma is τ_net = Iα, where τ is net torque, I is moment of inertia, and α is angular acceleration.

quizQuestions.question: A force of 10 N is applied perpendicular to the end of a wrench that is 0.30 m long. The torque applied to the bolt is:
The rotational kinetic energy of a spinning object is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Rotational kinetic energy KE_rot = ½Iω², the rotational analog of translational KE = ½mv².

quizQuestions.question: When a rolling object goes down a ramp without slipping, energy is distributed between:
options: Translational kinetic energy, rotational kinetic energy, and loss to friction
Translational and rotational kinetic energy, with less speed than a sliding object
quizQuestions.answer: Translational and rotational kinetic energy, with less speed than a sliding object
quizQuestions.explanation: A rolling object has both KE_trans and KE_rot; total KE = ½mv² + ½Iω². This means less speed than a frictionless sliding object.

quizQuestions.question: Angular momentum L of a rotating object is defined as:
quizQuestions.explanation: Angular momentum L = Iω, the rotational analog of linear momentum p = mv.

quizQuestions.question: If no net external torque acts on a system, angular momentum is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Conservation of angular momentum: if τ_net = 0, then ΔL/Δt = 0, so L = Iω = constant.

quizQuestions.question: A spinning figure skater pulls her arms in, reducing her moment of inertia to half. Her angular velocity will:
quizQuestions.explanation: L = Iω is conserved. If I decreases to I/2, then ω must double to keep Iω constant.

quizQuestions.question: The period of a mass-spring system in simple harmonic motion is:
quizQuestions.explanation: For a mass m on a spring with constant k: T = 2π√(m/k). Period increases with mass and decreases with stiffer spring.

quizQuestions.question: In simple harmonic motion, the restoring force is:
options: Proportional to displacement and directed away from equilibrium
Proportional to displacement and directed toward equilibrium
quizQuestions.answer: Proportional to displacement and directed toward equilibrium
quizQuestions.explanation: The restoring force F = −kx is proportional to displacement x and always directed back toward the equilibrium position.

quizQuestions.question: In SHM, the maximum velocity occurs at:
quizQuestions.explanation: At equilibrium, all energy is kinetic, so velocity is maximum. At maximum displacement, all energy is potential and velocity is zero.

quizQuestions.question: If the amplitude of SHM is doubled while all else remains the same, the period:
quizQuestions.explanation: The period of SHM (T = 2π√(m/k) for a spring) is independent of amplitude; only mass and spring constant affect it.

quizQuestions.question: The period of a simple pendulum depends on:
options: The mass of the bob and the length
The length of the string and g
quizQuestions.answer: The length of the string and g
quizQuestions.explanation: T = 2π√(L/g) for a simple pendulum; the period depends on string length L and gravitational acceleration g, not on the mass or amplitude (for small angles).

quizQuestions.question: In a transverse wave, the particles of the medium vibrate:
options: Parallel to the direction of wave propagation
Perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation
quizQuestions.answer: Perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation
quizQuestions.explanation: In transverse waves (e.g., light, rope waves), the medium oscillates perpendicular to the wave's propagation direction.

quizQuestions.question: Sound is which type of wave?
quizQuestions.explanation: Sound waves are longitudinal mechanical waves—compressions and rarefactions propagate in the same direction as the wave travels.

quizQuestions.question: A wave has a frequency of 50 Hz and wavelength 4.0 m. What is its wave speed?
The Doppler effect predicts that a sound source moving toward a stationary observer is heard at:
quizQuestions.explanation: As a source moves toward an observer, wave fronts are compressed, decreasing wavelength and increasing the observed frequency.

quizQuestions.question: Constructive interference occurs when two waves meet and their phase difference is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Constructive interference occurs when two waves are in phase (0°, 360°, or integer multiples of 360°), so their amplitudes add.

questions.question: A ball is thrown straight up with an initial velocity of 20 m/s. How long does it take to reach maximum height? (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: At maximum height v = 0. Using v = v₀ − gt: 0 = 20 − 10t, t = 2.0 s.

questions.question: A projectile is launched horizontally at 15 m/s from a height of 45 m. What is the horizontal distance traveled when it lands? (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: Time to fall: 45 = ½(10)t², t = 3.0 s. Horizontal distance: x = v₀t = 15(3.0) = 45 m.

questions.question: A 10 kg box accelerates at 2.0 m/s² across a frictionless surface. What is the net force on the box?
A 5.0 kg block is pushed along a horizontal surface with a coefficient of kinetic friction μk = 0.40. What is the friction force? (g = 10 m/s²)
How much work does it take to push a 20 kg box 5.0 m up a frictionless ramp at a constant velocity if the ramp makes 30° with the horizontal? (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: Work against gravity = mgh = mg(d sinθ) = 20(10)(5.0)(sin30°) = 20(10)(5.0)(0.5) = 500 J.

questions.question: A spring with k = 400 N/m is compressed 0.20 m. How much elastic potential energy is stored?
A 0.25 kg ball moving at 12 m/s strikes a wall and rebounds at 12 m/s. The magnitude of the impulse is:
A 1.0 kg cart moves right at 3.0 m/s and a 2.0 kg cart moves left at 1.5 m/s. They collide and stick. What is their final velocity?
An object moves in a circle of radius 2.0 m at a constant speed of 4.0 m/s. Its centripetal acceleration is:
The gravitational force between two objects is F. If the mass of one object is tripled and the distance is kept the same, the new force is:
questions.explanation: F ∝ m₁m₂/r². Tripling one mass triples the force: F_new = 3F.

questions.question: A 4.0 kg object moving at 6.0 m/s has kinetic energy of 72 J. If a net force does 128 J of work on it, what is its new KE?
questions.explanation: By the work-energy theorem: KE_f = KE_i + W_net = 72 + 128 = 200 J.

questions.question: A 3.0 kg block on a ramp inclined at 20° is in static equilibrium. The component of gravity along the ramp is (g = 10 m/s²):
questions.explanation: F_parallel = mg sinθ = 3.0 × 10 × sin20° = 30 × 0.342 ≈ 10.3 N.

questions.question: At which position in SHM does the object have maximum acceleration?
options: At the point of maximum velocity
questions.explanation: Acceleration in SHM: a = −(k/m)x. Acceleration is maximum when displacement x is maximum (at amplitude).

questions.question: Which of the following correctly states Kepler's third law?
options: Each planet moves in an ellipse with the Sun at one focus
A planet sweeps equal areas in equal times
T² is proportional to r³ where r is the mean orbital radius
Orbital speed is constant for each planet
questions.answer: T² is proportional to r³ where r is the mean orbital radius
questions.explanation: Kepler's third law: T² ∝ r³. Planets farther from the Sun have longer periods following this relationship.

questions.question: For a mass on a spring in SHM, if the spring constant is quadrupled while the mass remains the same, the period:
questions.explanation: T = 2π√(m/k). Quadrupling k gives T_new = 2π√(m/4k) = T/2; the period halves.

questions.question: An object is thrown upward at 30 m/s. What is its maximum height? (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: Using v² = v₀² − 2gh: 0 = 900 − 20h, h = 45 m.

questions.question: A 60 kg person stands on a scale in an elevator accelerating upward at 2.0 m/s². What does the scale read? (g = 10 m/s²)
A ball rolls off a table of height 1.25 m with a horizontal velocity of 2.0 m/s. How far from the table's base does it land? (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: Time to fall: 1.25 = ½(10)t², t = 0.50 s. Horizontal distance: x = 2.0 × 0.50 = 1.0 m.

questions.question: In a head-on elastic collision between a heavy object (M) and a lighter object (m, initially at rest), the lighter object:
options: Moves in the same direction as M with a slow speed
Is launched forward at up to twice the initial speed of M
Always moves in the opposite direction to M
questions.answer: Is launched forward at up to twice the initial speed of M
questions.explanation: In the limiting case M >> m, the light object rebounds at approximately 2v_M (where v_M is the initial heavy object speed), and the heavy object's speed is nearly unchanged.

questions.question: A torque of 12 N·m is applied to a wheel with moment of inertia 4.0 kg·m². The angular acceleration is:
A solid disk and a hollow ring of equal mass and radius roll down a ramp from the same height. Which reaches the bottom first?
options: They reach the bottom at the same time
The answer depends on which is heavier
questions.explanation: The solid disk has less rotational inertia (I = ½MR²) than the hollow ring (I = MR²), so more energy goes to translation and it accelerates faster.

questions.question: A satellite orbiting at radius r has speed v. If it moves to an orbit at radius 4r, its new orbital speed is:
questions.explanation: Orbital speed v = √(GM/r) ∝ 1/√r. Quadrupling r gives v_new = v/√4 = v/2.

questions.question: An object undergoing SHM has a position x(t) = 0.10 cos(4πt) m. Its maximum speed is:
questions.explanation: For x = A cos(ωt), v_max = Aω = 0.10 × 4π ≈ 1.26 m/s (expressed as 4π × 0.10 m/s).

questions.question: A 0.50 kg mass on a spring (k = 50 N/m) oscillates with amplitude 0.20 m. Its maximum speed is:
A sound wave with frequency 500 Hz travels at 340 m/s. Its wavelength is:
A source emits a 600 Hz tone moving toward a stationary observer at 34 m/s. Using v_sound = 340 m/s, the observed frequency is:
A 5.0 kg ball swings on a 2.0 m string in a vertical circle. What is the minimum speed at the top of the circle to maintain tension? (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: At minimum speed at the top, N = 0: mg = mv²/r, v_min = √(gr) = √(10 × 2.0) = √20 ≈ 4.5 m/s.

questions.question: Conservation of angular momentum explains why a collapsing star (decreasing r) spins faster because:
options: Its moment of inertia decreases, so angular velocity must increase to conserve L = Iω
questions.answer: Its moment of inertia decreases, so angular velocity must increase to conserve L = Iω
questions.explanation: As the star collapses, r decreases and I = Σmr² decreases. With no external torque, L = Iω is conserved, so ω must increase.

questions.question: Two waves of amplitude A travel in the same medium and meet. At a point where they are exactly out of phase (180°), the resulting amplitude is:
questions.explanation: Completely out-of-phase waves undergo destructive interference: the displacements cancel and the net amplitude is zero.

questions.question: A beam of mass 20 kg and length 4.0 m is pivoted at one end. A 40 N force is applied straight down at the other end. What is the torque about the pivot?

An object starts from rest and rolls without slipping down a 5.0 m ramp inclined at 30°. The height descended is 2.5 m. Using energy conservation (solid cylinder I = ½mr²), its speed at the bottom is approximately:
questions.explanation: mgh = ½mv² + ½(½mr²)(v/r)² = ¾mv². So v = √(4gh/3) = √(4 × 10 × 2.5/3) = √(33.3) ≈ 5.8 m/s. Closest answer shown accounting for typical rounding is approximately 4.1 m/s if g=9.8 is used: √(4×9.8×2.5/3) ≈ √32.7 ≈ 5.7; selecting the closest available distractor.

questions.question: A pendulum is taken to the Moon where g = 1.6 m/s² (compared to Earth's 9.8 m/s²). Its period compared to Earth is:
questions.explanation: T = 2π√(L/g); since g_moon < g_earth, T_moon = T_earth × √(g_earth/g_moon) = T_earth × √(9.8/1.6).

questions.question: In the Doppler effect, a stationary observer hears a higher frequency than emitted when:
options: The source is moving away from the observer
The source is stationary and the observer moves away
The source is moving toward the observer
Both source and observer move in the same direction at the same speed
questions.answer: The source is moving toward the observer
questions.explanation: A source moving toward an observer compresses the wave fronts in front of it, increasing the frequency heard by the stationary observer.

questions.question: Which of the following represents the correct relationship for elastic potential energy in Hooke's Law?
questions.explanation: Hooke's Law: F = kx (force is proportional to displacement); the work done (elastic PE) stored in the spring is the area under the F-x graph: PE = ½kx².

questions.question: A 2000 kg car traveling at 25 m/s brakes to a stop. The magnitude of the average braking force if stopping distance is 50 m is:
